excerpt_en: So, starting today, my activities in Funchal on the Madeira Islands are officially underway! By the way, the Madeira Islands are located here. Being on the southern side, the temperature is around 20 degrees, so many people are wearing short sleeves. https://you
body_en:
So, starting today,

my activities in Funchal on the Madeira Islands

are officially underway!

By the way, the Madeira Islands are located here.

Being on the southern side,

the temperature is around 20 degrees,

so many people are wearing short sleeves.

https://youtu.be/SGxDegmP_X8?si=pfjBeeFOAZmCzGnm

Today there's a Madeira wine tour in the evening,

so until then, I explored the town.

Right now, most European countries are in the middle of winter,

and many people come here seeking a warm place.

It's kind of like Okinawa for Japan, I guess.

For Europeans,

it's an easy place to get to.

On the other hand, it's barely known

to Asians, including Japanese people.

As for the reasons,

there are several factors to consider.

1. Geographical Distance and Access Issues

  • It's far away: The Madeira Islands are Portuguese territory, a group of islands floating in the Atlantic Ocean. There are no direct flights from Japan, and you need to transfer through Lisbon or major European cities, making it feel like a high hurdle to visit.
  • Awareness as a tourist destination: While it's a popular tourist destination in Europe, there's hardly any advertising or promotion in Asia or Japan, so awareness is low.

2. Lack of Information in Japan

  • Insufficient tourism information: Guidebooks and travel websites rarely feature Madeira, limiting opportunities to learn about it.
  • Lack of media exposure: Since it's rarely covered on TV or in magazines, the average Japanese person isn't familiar with it.

3. Biased Image of Portuguese Tourist Destinations

  • Focus on Lisbon and Porto: When people think of Portuguese tourism, cities like Lisbon, Porto, and Sintra are famous, and the Madeira Islands get buried as a "resort far from the mainland."
  • Japanese travel tendencies: For European trips, popular destinations like France, Italy, and Spain take priority, and since Portugal itself isn't that well-known, Madeira, being part of it, stands out even less.

4. Differences from Japanese Travelers' Preferences

  • History and culture orientation: Japanese tourists tend to prefer ruins and historical landmarks, but Madeira is more of a place to enjoy nature and outdoor activities.
  • Comparison with Hawaii and Okinawa: It's common to go to closer resort destinations from Japan (Hawaii, Okinawa, Maldives), and distant resorts like Madeira don't easily make the list.

5. Impact of Portugal's Tourism Strategy

  • Insufficient focus on the Asian market: It's only recently that the Portuguese government and tourism industry have been strengthening their promotion to Asian markets including Japan. Until now, their main targets were within Europe and America.

The Madeira Islands are still an "unknown resort" for Japanese people, but their natural beauty and unique experiences could potentially become attractive to Japanese people in the future.

I actually knew about the Madeira Islands because I had the opportunity to handle Madeira wine during my days as a hotel worker.

Of course,

I only thought of it as wine from a faraway country.

But,

actually coming here, it's incredibly great!

It's really like Okinawa or Hawaii,

and even though it's only been one day, it's super comfortable!

And what makes it really obvious is,

there are no Asians here.

When I was in Porto, I did see Koreans and Chinese people. I didn't see a single Japanese person though.

But in Madeira,

I don't even see any Asians at all lol

As I wrote in this article,

Madeira has hardly done

any promotion in Asia,

which is probably why things are like this.

So even if you search online,

barely any information comes up lol

I'm the type who can enjoy this kind of environment,

so I'm not particularly anxious or anything.

Walking around the town is really fun.

After exploring the town,

it was time for the winery visit.

Heading to Blandy's Wine Lodge

We went to Blandy's Wine Lodge,

the oldest winery in Madeira.

Of course, the goal was

to see if FOMUS masu could do some kind of collaboration.

This time too, I joined a tour-style visit,

and they taught me about the Madeira wine production process.

Do you all know the difference between Port wine and Madeira wine?

I studied it back in my hotel worker days,

but this time, I understood it again from scratch.

About the Differences

Madeira wine and Port wine are both fortified wines originating from Portugal, but there are major differences in their production regions, manufacturing methods, and flavors.

1. Difference in Production Region

  • Madeira wine: Produced on the Madeira Islands, floating in the Atlantic Ocean about 1,000 km from the Portuguese mainland.
  • Port wine: Produced in the Douro Valley (northern region) of mainland Portugal.

2. Differences in the Production Process

Madeira Wine

  • Heating process: Aged through the unique Estufagem (heating wine in a greenhouse) or Canteiro (natural heating) methods.
  • Heating produces caramel and nutty flavors, along with distinctive oxidized flavors.
  • Aging method: Emphasizing heating and oxidation, the flavor becomes more complex over time.
  • Preservation: Resistant to deterioration even after opening, allowing for long-term storage.

Port Wine

  • Halting fermentation: Grape spirits (brandy) are added during fermentation to stop the process, leaving sweetness.
  • Aging method: Aged in wooden barrels (with less oxidation). The style is determined by the duration and method of barrel aging.
  • Ruby Port: Fresh and fruity taste.
  • Tawny Port: Nutty and caramel flavors from barrel aging.
  • Vintage Port: Deep complexity from bottle aging.

3. Differences in Taste

  • Madeira wine:
  • Characterized by high acidity, with good balance whether dry or sweet.
  • Strong flavors of caramel, nuts, dried fruits, and spices from the heating process.
  • Stable flavors from long-term aging.
  • Port wine:
  • Rich in fruit flavors, especially strong sweetness.
  • Centered on fruit flavors like plum, berry, and cherry.
  • Tawny varieties also have nutty and caramel flavors.

4. Differences in Style

Madeira Wine Styles:

  • Wide range from dry to sweet.
  • *You can choose from Sercial (dry) to Malmsey (sweet)**.
  • All styles are characterized by acidity.

Port Wine Styles:

  • Primarily sweet.
  • Ruby Port (fresh), Tawny Port (aged), and Vintage Port (grapes from special years) are representative.
  • Dry Port wine (White Port) is rare.

6. Preservation

  • Madeira wine: Resistant to oxidation after opening, can be stored for months to years.
  • Port wine: Oxidizes easily after opening, recommended to drink within a few weeks.

7. Historical Background

  • Madeira wine: During the Age of Exploration, it was discovered that heating and oxidation during long-distance shipping improved quality, leading to the current production methods.
  • Port wine: Due to conflict between England and France, England promoted Portuguese wine, leading to its growing popularity.

So even though they're both fortified wines, their production methods are completely different.

This time too, I chose a course with tasting,

Naturally,

the color, aroma, and alcohol content are all completely different.

Port wine is all sweet,

while Madeira wine ranges from dry to sweet,

which is also distinctive.

This time, I tried three types,

and each had a completely different aroma,

which made the tasting really enjoyable.

Of course, I tried masu x Madeira wine too. While the glass-specific aromas don't bloom the same way, in terms of the experience of drinking from a wooden vessel, the hinoki didn't interfere with the wine that much, so it might be possible to pair it with Madeira wine.

Also, the souvenir corner

was very small-scale,

It was at this level.

If I could place masu with the Blandy's logo here,

they could work as gifts too,

so I'm planning to make a proposal.

Like this,

I'm always thinking about how to get people to use masu,

and looking at everything from that perspective.

If they could include them as a branded wooden box option in their tour packages, they could work as mini gifts, so I think it could be a good idea!

Anyway, I'll put out the offer and see.

This is the kind of thing I do every time I visit a winery.

This time too, everyone besides me was elderly European couples.

Actually, the Madeira Islands in general

are full of elderly Europeans! lol

Not many young people around.

Whether it's because it's a weekday,

or because young people head out to Lisbon or Porto,

the town is basically all elderly folks.

So doing Masu Photo shoots in Madeira

would be pretty difficult.

*No photo shoots planned for this trip

*I left my camera behind too

After the winery visit,

I went for another stroll around town.

Having drunk three glasses of wine,

with the warm weather outside,

and being sleep-deprived,

I wasn't getting drunk exactly,

but it was really comfortable,

so I bought what's probably a traditional dish -- meat stuffed in bread.

When I showed this to ChatGPT,

it told me it was one of the local dishes commonly found on the Madeira Islands, called "Bolo do Caco com Carne de Vinho e Alhos (Bolo do Caco with wine and garlic braised meat)"!

Anyway, the meat was incredibly tender

and easy to eat.

Then, there was also the custard tart, a sweet you can find anywhere in Portugal.

Honestly, since coming to Portugal,

I've been living an extremely high-calorie lifestyle.

This year, I've been drinking a ton of beer in Ireland too, drinking wine almost every day in Portugal, eating calorie-bomb sandwiches,

I'm seriously going to get fat.

Actually, I filmed YouTube today too,

and I think my face has gotten a bit chubbier.

As someone who works as a photographer and does brand sales,

I need to be trusted by people from any country,

and it's essential to maintain an approachable look, so I'll make sure to take care of my body.

Heading to a recommended wine bar

I went to a wine bar that was recommended to me at the craft beer place yesterday.

This place carries wines other than Madeira wine too, and I had a regular wine for the first time in a while.

Yeah, it was incredibly delicious.

The owner was very kind

and let me taste

three types of wine.

I was actually thinking of going to

the craft beer place from yesterday after this,

but at this point,

I'd already had four glasses of wine and was full,

so there's no need to force more drinks,

and I'll just swing by again tomorrow.

Besides, it would be hard to place

masu at this wine bar lol

*Always thinking about expanding FOMUS

excerpt_en: So, activities in Madeira continue. That said, I don't have any outdoor activities planned today. Instead, I've got online meetings and things I need to get done by the end of this week piled up. Working on the international version of the support site for the Sagishima Gallery project being developed in Japan,
body_en:
So, activities in Madeira continue.

That said, I don't have any outdoor activities planned today.

Instead, I've got online meetings and

things I need to get done by the end of this week

piled up.

Working on the international version of the support site

for the Sagishima Gallery project being developed in Japan,

as well as operational preparations.

Plus, meetings about the artworks

to be exhibited at the gallery

and design discussions.

Different people are involved in each,

and even within the same project,

the meeting members can be different.

The operations team and the creator team

have different things to ask of them,

so the communication changes too.

Also, separately from that,

to keep the FOMUS brand going,

I'm not just earning money

but also raising funds.

This isn't with FOMUS members,

but with a completely different team.

This kind of stuff doesn't really show on the surface,

but I've been constantly doing leader duties [gathering People, Products, and Money].

From the outside,

it might look like I'm just playing around overseas.

I get that it can look that way to some extent.

But my work

isn't painful or unpleasant,

it's tough but rewarding.

And nobody else is doing it.

Nobody would normally come all the way to the Madeira Islands

just to spread masu lol

By the way, I sent an email to

Blandy's, where I did the tour visit yesterday.

*I went all out on the materials

The chances of getting a reply are low,

but having sent it, the chances aren't zero.

If I don't hear back by tomorrow,

I'm thinking of going to the shop and delivering the materials in person.

Since I'm doing stuff like this every day,

it might be a bit different from a normal life,

but if you think of it as playing the game of business,

life is actually pretty interesting.

Walking around town

In the end, all the meetings finished

at 3 PM.

From there, I headed out to grab some food.

Beer is a must,

along with deep-fried square rice cakes

and a fish burger.

Both are apparently local Madeira dishes.

They tasted good.

But all that for 2,500 yen! lol

That's a pretty decent price lol

After that, I took a light stroll around town

and worked in my room.

Just because I'm in the Madeira Islands

doesn't mean I can be outside playing all the time.

But tomorrow,

I've booked a tour of Funchal,

so while keeping a business perspective,

I'm going to enjoy the tour.

excerpt_en: So, today is Madeira scenic tour DAY! A tour, of all things. lol There's a reason for this -- when going to tourist spots, normally I'd take a bus or train and go by myself, but the Madeira Islands don't have that infrastructure in place yet, and I found out that the tourist spots I wanted to see
body_en:
So, today is Madeira scenic tour DAY!

A tour, of all things. lol

There's a reason for this --

when going to tourist spots, normally

I'd take a bus or train and go by myself,

but the Madeira Islands

don't have that infrastructure in place yet,

and I found out that the tourist spots I wanted to see

couldn't be reached by public transportation,

so just this once, I decided to use a tour.

It was about an 8-hour tour for under 6,000 yen, so I chose a relatively affordable plan.

A group of about 15 people got on a bus

and we were shown around.

Naturally,

everyone else came with their families or partners,

and I was the only solo player. lol

I was the only Asian too.

Everyone else came from across Europe.

The first place we headed to was

PICO DO ARIEIRO, the third highest mountain in Madeira. Apparently it's 1,800 meters tall.

Photos don't quite do it justice.

So, I've recorded it on YouTube as a video -- after you finish reading this article,

feel free to check it out at 2x speed.

https://youtu.be/zjFv4lJ4IEM?si=QWzh22LtFNwE571H

The weather was absolutely perfect,

and the views were insanely stunning.

It's great being above the clouds.

At this point, I'd already gotten my money's worth lol

Next, we headed to a spot called Silva.

Speaking of Silva,

FOMUS made

an original card game also called SILVA.

SILVA means "forest,"

so well, it's probably a name you'd hear anywhere.

By the way, the "Madeira" in Madeira Islands

means "wood."

For FOMUS,

the Madeira Islands

have a lot of connections.

Our character "KUKU" is also a wood character,

and this Madeira expedition also serves

as a scouting trip related to that.

Also, one great thing about tours is

that they stop at tourist spots for you.

This was a place called Faial.

We drove along the lower roads while also climbing way up high to take in the views.

And then, we headed to Santana.

Thatched roofs! Would you believe it.

I looked it up afterward and found out

that thatched roofs exist all around the world,

though with different materials.

I had assumed they only existed in Japan,

but it turns out

there are quite a variety of them.

Fun facts

There are several differences between Japanese thatched roofs and the thatched roofs of Santana's traditional houses (Casas Tipicas de Santana) on Madeira Island.

1. Roof Shape

  • Japanese thatched roofs:
  • Mainly hip-roof (yosemune-zukuri) or gable-roof (kirizuma-zukuri) styles, with a relatively gentle roof angle.
  • In regions with heavy snow or rain, steeper roofs exist (e.g., gassho-zukuri).
  • The houses themselves are large, with wide roofs being characteristic.
  • Santana thatched roofs:
  • *A-frame (steep triangular shape)**, where the roof itself essentially forms the structure of the house.
  • The roof slope is very steep, designed for rainwater to flow off easily.

2. Materials Used

  • Japanese thatched roofs:
  • Use pampas grass, reed (yoshi), and cogon grass.
  • Some regions also use kaya grass or straw.
  • Santana thatched roofs:
  • Use *local reed grass** and palm family plants.
  • Materials suited to Portugal's warm climate.

3. Structure and Walls

  • Japanese thatched roofs:
  • Under the roof, wooden pillars and beams are solidly assembled.
  • Walls are made of earthen walls or plaster (shikkui), providing durability.
  • Santana thatched roofs:
  • Most of the house is roof, with minimal walls.
  • Walls are made of stone, wood, and plaster, painted in colorful colors.
  • Overall simpler construction compared to Japanese thatched roofs.

4. Living Environment and Climate Adaptation

  • Japanese thatched roofs:
  • Adapted to Japan's humid climate with large temperature differences.
  • Thick construction keeps it cool in summer and warm in winter.
  • Roofs require regular maintenance (sashi-kaya/patching).
  • Santana thatched roofs:
  • Designed for Madeira Island's warm oceanic climate.
  • Simpler than Japanese versions, prioritizing ventilation over insulation.

5. Cultural Background

  • Japanese thatched roofs:
  • Have a long tradition and are preserved as gassho-zukuri houses (Shirakawa-go) and old folk houses.
  • Once common residences in rural areas, but declining in modern times.
  • Santana thatched roofs:
  • Historical farmhouses on Madeira Island, now preserved for tourism.
  • Landscape conservation is carried out as buildings symbolizing traditional culture.

Summary

| Comparison | Japanese Thatched Roofs | Santana Thatched Roofs |

| --- | --- | --- |

| Shape | Gentle slope, gassho-zukuri, etc. | A-frame triangular |

| Materials | Pampas grass, reed, straw | Reed, palm family plants |

| Wall Structure | Wood + earthen wall/plaster | Wood + stone/plaster (colorful) |

| Climate Adaptation | High insulation | Ventilation-focused |

| Cultural Role | Traditional farmhouses, tourism resources | Preserved for tourism |

Both are traditional architectural styles with long histories that adapted to their regional environments, but the differences in roof shape and climate adaptation are particularly significant.

So

lots of tourists

gather in this town called Santana.

Apparently only about 7,000 people live here now.

Since lots of people gather here too,

if I could place masu as souvenirs here,

that would be huge.

Lunch time

Lunch was optional -- those who wanted could go to a designated restaurant.

Since I was alone anyway,

I figured I'd take the chance to mingle with the other participants,

so I chose the tour lunch.

It was a round table,

so we introduced ourselves, drank wine,

and ate food.

The wine was all-you-can-drink,

and the food was delicious, so all good!

Since I was a guy who signed up for the tour alone,

I got bombarded with questions. lol

This too might be

the fate of a solo player.

That said,

I've got an endless supply of stories to tell,

so no problems there lol

After that, we moved on further,

heading to the easternmost point.

Anyway, the weather was great and the scenery was amazing.

As I mentioned on YouTube, Ireland also has a famous World Heritage cliff, but weather aside, this one was overwhelmingly better lol

Also, these cliffs have no fences or barriers at all,

so you can easily fall off. lol

I was filming YouTube near the edge,

slipped and fell,

and one wrong step would have meant death.

I'll really be more careful...

And then, we headed to Machico.

Here, instead of walking around the city,

it was a look-down-from-above style.

This was also incredibly beautiful,

and I felt it was a perfect match for

the worldview of FOMUS's character "KUKU,"

so it was a great learning experience.

So that's how it went --

a tour that covered about 4 locations in one day.

Personally,

I got multiple ideas for KUKU's world, learned about Madeira's souvenirs,

and it was an extremely productive time.

Also, talking with tour participants from all over Europe, I realized that Japanese people are special -- they watch our anime and dramas, and they happily chat about it. And the fact that I can communicate in English means this past year in an English-speaking environment has really made a huge difference.

If someone asked me "what if this were a private trip?"

I'd say I'd want to come with a partner or family.

*Doing a group tour solo is lonely, man

Heading to the craft beer place

After the tour ended,

I went back to the hotel once

to edit the video I shot during the tour.

It's less about wanting lots of people to see it,

and more that I genuinely want to preserve this record.

Then, I sent sales emails to Port wine wineries in Porto and Madeira wine wineries in Madeira, replied to those who had responded, and did other administrative work.

After finishing most of the work,

I made my second visit to

the craft beer shop I randomly found on my first day.

This place has different beers every day,

so it's somewhere I'd want to visit daily,

and the owner is a really great person, making it a comfortable place to be.

The owner told me this shop

has been open for 10 years.

I ended up staying for about 2 hours

and drank 4 beers.

By the end I was the only one left in the shop,

and for some reason they gave me a ton of snacks lol

From morning till late at night,

it was an action-packed day full of outdoor activities!

title_en: What You Learn from Visiting Wineries
excerpt_en: So, another day of activities in Madeira. During the day, I was working on materials for the Sagishima gallery opening announcement and crowdfunding launch scheduled for midnight on the 18th. On top of that, preparing collaboration materials for FOMUS and sending emails to wineries I liked while touring Portuguese wineries.
body_en:
So, another day of activities in Madeira.

During the day, I was working on materials

for the Sagishima gallery opening announcement and crowdfunding launch release, scheduled for midnight on the 18th.

On top of that, preparing FOMUS collaboration materials and sending emails to the wineries I liked while touring Portuguese wineries.

It's a low-key activity,

but if I don't create these emails and materials,

there's no point in me going to the wineries in person.

So for me, it's really important.

Now, there are multiple Madeira wine wineries,

and this time I went to H. M. Borges - Madeira Wine.

Wine tour at H. M. Borges

The wine tour was in the evening.

The price was relatively cheap,

so I was wondering what it'd be like,

but compared to other wineries it was small-scale and wasn't set up as a tourist tour venue.

Until now, the wineries in Porto and Madeira

had visitor flow designed for tourists coming in, but this one didn't.

So the projector was

barely projecting onto a white wall.

Other places had automatic setups,

projected onto wine barrels,

and projectors were mounted

rather than just placed on the floor.

It's a winery with over 200 years of history,

but they didn't specialize in that presentation aspect,

and it felt like they were

competing on wine quality instead.

And then, tasting time.

It was a comparison of 3-year and 10-year wines,

with the same grape variety prepared for both.

At the previous Blandy's,

the changes in taste and aroma were clearly noticeable,

but honestly, I couldn't tell the difference here.

If anything, the 3-year one had better aroma and taste.

Personally, when it comes to taste,

I preferred Port wine

over Madeira wine.

*Just a matter of preference

Also, this shop didn't have much explanation during tasting, so I really had to just rely on my own senses to figure things out.

Additionally, the snack presentation was weak.

When you've been visiting winery after winery, these differences really start to stand out lol

So I tried placing a masu there,

but it was a bit underwhelming lol

Heading to the craft beer shop (3rd time)

Then, after going back to the hotel and finishing all my work, I headed to the craft beer shop once again.

The owner here is kind,

it's just a 10-minute walk from my accommodation,

and I can drink delicious craft beer.

Plus, while one visit alone isn't enough,

if I'm going to do some business in Madeira in the future,

I want to be on good terms with someone from Madeira who's been running a business here for over 10 years.

It makes it easier to consult when something comes up.

The beer being delicious is of course a reason too, but equally, I'm a business owner.

To expand a brand globally,

it ultimately comes down to "people,"

so these kinds of activities are incredibly important.

Meeting face to face over and over again

is, when it comes down to it, actually a shortcut.

So that's it --

my Madeira stay ends tomorrow!

excerpt_en: So, it's the last day of the Madeira expedition. That said, I didn't leave Funchal, just explored the town. There are tons of souvenir shops, so I did reconnaissance and kept watching what customers were buying. Places that seemed useful for reference,
body_en:
So, it's the last day of the Madeira expedition.

That said, I didn't leave Funchal,

just explored the town.

There are tons of souvenir shops, so I did reconnaissance and

kept watching what customers were buying.

I took photos of places that seemed useful for reference

and compiled them.

It's not confirmed yet,

and it hasn't even started,

but I think I've mentioned a few times that

I'm planning to create a souvenir brand

aimed at global expansion starting spring 2025 or later,

and there's a lot I need to observe for that.

Most souvenir shops

sell the same things,

so no matter where you buy, the products are the same.

Plus, tourists are the ones buying,

and getting someone to think "I want to buy from this person!" is hard when you're meeting them for the first time at a souvenir shop.

So what do you do about that?

Figuring that out is also my job.

Since only paid subscribers are reading this, I'll write it here:

If FOMUS is going to do this,

we'd collaborate with Madeira and Portuguese designers.

Influential people with top-tier design skills.

Add them to the team,

create a product together,

and sell it together.

If you try to sell something in Madeira with just the Japanese team, it won't work.

Because

there's nothing in it for the locals.

Also, I don't think placing FOMUS content in regular souvenir shops is the right move either. In Madeira, I'd sell at shops that have become tourist attractions in their own right.

Director (FOMUS)

Designer (local designer)

Retail (shops at tourist spots)

That's the kind of setup I'd put together.

Of course, this requires costs,

but if you're going to properly create

selling opportunities,

you invest in team building.

Considering marketing too,

it'll actually be cheaper this way in the end.

Right now, I'm traveling around the world

visiting tourist spots, connecting with local people --

gathering the elements FOMUS will need going forward

by seeing things with my own feet.

It's hard to make a big impact

with just a few days of staying somewhere.

I'm not strong enough yet

to close deals on the spot.

But I am building "connections" that money can never buy.

These won't create some huge impact right away either -- it's about whether you keep at it steadily.

My role right now is

to see the world,

absorb everything I can,

and use it

to build a brand that can compete internationally.

Of course, there are people who'd say

"You'd sell more if you just stayed in Japan

and worked at it steadily."

But I want to see the wider world first,

compete on the global stage,

and expand the brand.

Business-wise, I think if we don't start building overseas customers, we won't be able to compete in the coming era.

In Japan,

Fuji TV's credibility has plummeted rapidly,

and in America,

Trump created a meme coin

and made massive profits in just a single day.

Times keep moving forward,

and I feel like only those who adapt well

will survive.

I've been setting my direction by reading the trends all along.

The fact that FOMUS

has survived for about 4 years is exactly because of that.

Constantly gathering information,

going to meet people,

joining communities,

and showing the process all the way to results.

It's this cycle, over and over.

I've always told the people around me,

"I want to do this kind of thing!"

I haven't done everything yet,

but I've been bringing them to life one by one.

You can't just be a big talker --

a professional delivers results.

I went off on a tangent,

but the conclusion is,

I can't move to the next step

unless I grow as a person.

I'm turning 30 in 2025.

I can get a Working Holiday visa

until I'm 31.

During this period, I'll basically be active overseas,

improving my English skills,

making lots of friends and companions,

and aiming to grow the community.

A community connected by "people" rather than money, an environment connected by "masu."

I'll do whatever it takes for that.

Last beers

My last stop in Madeira was

the craft beer shop I visited first when I arrived.

In the end, I came here 4 out of my 6 days here lol

Today I went in right at opening,

and because of the bus schedule to the airport, I only stayed for 10 minutes.

And as a token of gratitude for our encounter,

I gifted them a FOMUS masu.

If anyone ever goes to the Madeira Islands,

please visit Fuga.

That wraps up the Madeira Islands expedition

The Madeira expedition ends here.

Visiting 2 Madeira wine producers,

scouting souvenir shops in Funchal city,

scouting other tourist spots (Santana, etc.),

gathering materials for KUKU's world,

connecting with local people,

checking which demographics are traveling here

and so on --

it was an extremely productive time as a scouting trip.

Actually, during this period,

I heard back from

Blandy's Madeira Wine,

and got connected to a Madeira wine company in Japan.

It might still be a while before we see results, but I believe that by steadily making progress, I'll come across a big opportunity.

And whether I can seize that opportunity or not

depends on how quickly I can act in my daily activities.

Getting stronger right now

is an absolute necessity,

and on top of that,

building even one more local connection.

That's what I keep doing relentlessly on these overseas expeditions.

Just like in Porto,

I didn't encounter a single Japanese person in Madeira either.

That's how few Japanese people

are currently active overseas.

That alone is an advantage,

and if you haven't gone abroad yet,

I really recommend trying it at least once.

Alright, time for my flight.

Next up is Lisbon, the capital of Portugal.

I'll only be there for 3 days,

but I already have some plans lined up,

and I'm hoping to land at least one business connection.

So with that,

the Madeira Islands expedition arc is complete!

excerpt_en: So, starting today I'm active in Lisbon. That said, it's only about 3 days, a short stay. My goals in Lisbon are: scouting souvenirs at tourist spots, scouting alcohol-related things in Lisbon, building connections with locals. Trying to pack in as much as I can,
body_en:
So, starting today I'm active in Lisbon.

That said, it's only about 3 days, a short stay.

My goals in Lisbon are:

  • Scouting souvenirs at tourist spots.
  • Scouting alcohol-related things in Lisbon
  • Building connections with locals

That's the plan.

To pack in as much as possible,

I've set no online meetings for this period,

and all consultations are being handled through messages.

*Except for emergencies

So,

I headed out to explore the city from the morning.

Lisbon has multiple tourist spots,

but compared to other countries,

I personally don't find them that attractive.

There's the Lisbon Cathedral and castle,

and tons of souvenir shops near them.

So I did some scouting.

Speaking of Portugal,

it's all about Azulejo (azulejo).

In Porto, Madeira, and Lisbon,

everywhere, it's Azulejo.

In other words, tiles.

Souvenirs of those are lined up everywhere.

Plus, sardines are famous,

so there are plush sardine toys too.

The souvenirs in Porto and Lisbon are almost the same.

Put another way, that's all there is.

Japanese souvenirs differ

from region to region,

but Portugal's are pretty much the same. lol

This is also a big learning point,

and the new brand FOMUS will launch

will be released in a completely different format

from these,

and it might actually be fine

to not even have a "Lisbon feel" to it.

As long as we can create the situation where "you can only buy it in Lisbon," that's enough.

While walking around, I found something that looked like a traditional local food, so I bought it without knowing what it was.

This is

a traditional Portuguese dish called

"Pastel de Bacalhau."

It's a croquette-like dish made with salted cod (bacalhau), mixed with potatoes, eggs, parsley, and onions, then deep-fried.

Its characteristic elongated oval shape features a crispy outside and moist inside.

Recently, the "Pastel de Bacalhau com Queijo" version, stuffed with cheese (Queijo Serra) from the Serra da Estrela region, has also become popular.

The one I ate had this cheese inside,

and it was incredibly delicious.

But it cost 5 euros! lol

That's about 800-900 yen.

A seriously premium croquette lol

I had joined a Portugal community on Facebook beforehand

Actually, before coming to Portugal,

I had joined a Japanese expats in Portugal group on Facebook.

I thought I might be able to get some useful information there.

It said self-introductions were welcome, so I posted about my activities and asked "Anyone want to grab drinks?"

and got responses from two people.

Of course, my mission is to get as many people as possible to know about the FOMUS brand,

so I met both of them today.

First up, at lunch.

She's a Japanese woman who's lived in France for about 25 years, running a business providing Japanese sake and other Japanese alcoholic beverages in France.

She said she reached out because

I had mentioned "masu" in my self-introduction.

What's more, she was in Lisbon on a business trip,

and today was her day to fly back to France.

We talked about all sorts of things,

and she said she loves traditional Japanese craftsmanship,

and since many people have already been to Japan,

there are lots of people who really love Japan.

For FOMUS too,

to deliver masu to more people,

it's easier to target people who already love Japan,

so I genuinely started considering France

as a Working Holiday destination for summer and beyond this year.

Of course, since I haven't seen it firsthand myself,

I can't say for sure,

but I know Japanese content is doing really well in Paris, so it might be good for business.

My current Working Holiday candidates are Paris, Berlin, Czech Republic, and Hungary.

Getting back on track a bit --

since the other person also runs a sake business

and works with multiple Japanese prefectures,

she's a full-on business owner.

Naturally, she knows way more than me,

and she taught me a lot.

At the same time, there are things

only someone like me who's been flying around the world would understand, so we shared information back and forth.

By the way,

lunch was an all-out Portuguese feast!

Since there were two of us,

the advantage was being able to share all sorts of dishes.

Grilled Sardines (Sardinhas Assadas)

A signature Portuguese dish where fresh sardines are grilled and typically served with olive oil, garlic, parsley, and red onion. The side of potatoes is also traditional.

Shrimp in Tomato Sauce (Gambas a Guilho / Gambas com Tomate)

Shrimp cooked in garlic and tomato sauce. Sometimes spicy, and typically eaten with bread.

Octopus Risotto (Arroz de Polvo)

One of Portugal's traditional dishes, with tender octopus cooked and prepared with rice in a risotto style. Topped with coriander (cilantro), it's packed with the umami of seafood.

All popular Portuguese seafood dishes.

I enjoy good food,

but this kind of food I want to eat with someone else,

so I hadn't had any during this expedition.

And now,

I got to enjoy the finest Portuguese cuisine.

Plus, white wine too! lol

It was an extremely productive time.

Also, since she had time before her flight,

we continued chatting at her hotel lounge.

Evening meeting too

And in the evening, I met the second person.

I only knew their name beforehand,

but when we actually talked,

it turned out they were from North Carolina in the US, living in Lisbon, and could speak a bit of Japanese.

I didn't ask their age,

but they seemed around my age or a bit younger.

We talked about business, politics,

Japanese and American culture and more,

for about 3 hours.

For me, this kind of time is really important

because I can truly understand the other person.

They spoke native English,

but it was very easy to understand,

and even with my still-developing English skills,

I could follow well.

According to them,

even though they'd sent messages to people

in the Facebook community,

they'd never actually gone out for drinks before.

Apparently people like me who broadcast "Let's go drinking!"

to a large group are rare.

Plus, my strength is

that I'm extremely quick on my feet.

And I'm acting solo.

As a result, encounters like this happen.

Meeting these two people

wouldn't have happened if I hadn't joined

the Portugal group on Facebook.

You never know what life has in store.

In the end, your actions

can change the future in any number of ways.

I find that incredibly exciting,

and I keep getting new inspiration.

Truly, I'm grateful for all of it.

So today,

I was blessed with the opportunity

to connect with two people.

What a fulfilling time, right from day one in Lisbon.

Tomorrow and the day after, I don't have specific plans,

so I'll explore the city a bit more

and maybe stop by a Japanese-run restaurant

that someone in the Facebook group told me about.

Honestly, I don't particularly crave Japanese food,

but scouting is the goal.

Let's keep at it!

title_en: Thoughts While Walking Through the Rainy City
excerpt_en: Day 2 in Lisbon. I woke up to a gloomy sky outside the window. The patter of rain made for a somewhat depressing day. Lisbon is a city with beautiful outdoor scenery. On a sunny day, you could climb the hills and look out over the cityscape, or take a leisurely tram ride,
body_en:
Day 2 in Lisbon.

I woke up to a gloomy sky outside the window. The patter of rain made for a somewhat depressing day.

Lisbon is a city with beautiful outdoor scenery. On a sunny day, you could climb the hills and look out over the cityscape, or take a leisurely tram ride, but today wasn't going to be one of those days.

With no other choice, I decided to stroll around and check out souvenir shops.

Touring Lisbon's Souvenir Shops

Lisbon's souvenir shops are full of tile-pattern goods. Portugal is famous for its tile culture called "Azulejo," and items like coasters, magnets, and tableware in tile patterns fill the shelves.

There are also some handmade craft shops with intricate tile designs that, looking at them, remind me somewhat of Japanese traditional craftsmanship. I'm personally really drawn to this kind of artisan-driven merchandise.

But while browsing souvenir shops is fun, when it comes to "should I actually buy something?" it gets tricky. Small items are fine, but large ceramics are hard to carry home, and they're pricey too.

So in the end, it was just a "window shopping" kind of tour.

TIMEOUT Market and Portuguese Prices

For lunch, I stopped by the "TIMEOUT Market."

This is a food market that's famous as a tourist spot. Popular restaurants from all over Portugal have stalls here, and it's supposed to be a place where you can enjoy all kinds of cuisine... but the prices shocked me.

"15 euros (~2,500 yen) and up"

Wait, those are regular lunch prices...!?

If I were in the mood to splurge with "it's a trip, let's treat ourselves!" that'd be fine, but I passed this time.

Basically, I'm the type who "only eats something nice when dining with someone else."

My company isn't that big yet, so I try to keep food costs down while spending money on connecting with people.

Sharing a good meal with someone has value, but 2,500 yen for a solo lunch is a bit too extravagant for me right now.

Sheltering from Rain in Lisbon and Thinking

The rain kept getting heavier, so I took shelter in a shopping mall.

It's an expedition and all, but bad weather limits what you can do. So I pulled out my phone and got some work done.

Trump's presidential inauguration is today too.

Politics aside, the news made me curious about Bitcoin's price movement.

I diversify my assets not just in cash but also in crypto. As part of risk management, I always keep an eye on how different markets are affected.

I thought this news might cause a big move, but in the end, there was barely any change.

Moments like these remind me that it's important not to just watch price movements, but to think from a broader perspective about "how is the world changing right now?"

The Shock of Rice-less Curry

For dinner, I had curry.

The menu said "Curry: 7 euros (~1,100 yen)," so I thought "Oh, that's cheap!" and ordered.

...But when the plate arrived, I was shocked.

"There's no rice."

Apparently, the curry sauce alone was 7 euros.

"Wait, curry without rice -- isn't that just sauce...?"

In the end, adding rice was +4 euros (~650 yen). Plus a beer, and the total came to 14 euros (~2,200 yen).

"What was the point of skipping TIMEOUT Market...?" I thought, while experiencing firsthand the difficulty of food costs in Lisbon.

And so, day 2 came to a (?) successful end.

Here's hoping for sunshine next time.

Summary: How to Spend a Rainy Day in Lisbon

  • Touring Lisbon's souvenir shops -- Lots of tile goods, with a charm similar to Japanese traditional craftsmanship
  • TIMEOUT Market -- Looks delicious but tourist prices (lunch from 15 euros)
  • Sheltering from rain in a shopping mall -- Checked Trump's inauguration and Bitcoin price movements
  • The unexpected rice-less curry incident at dinner -- Ended up spending 2,200 yen on a single meal

Next time, I want to enjoy Lisbon in good weather!

This text was written by me and fleshed out by ChatGPT.

title_en: The Portugal Expedition Comes to an End
excerpt_en: Last day in Lisbon! Miraculously, cloudy with occasional sunshine! It had been raining nonstop until now, so not getting rained on alone is a win! First, I headed to Lisbon's fab lab. Major cities around the world have maker spaces where you can use 3D printers,
body_en:
Last day in Lisbon!

Miraculously, cloudy with occasional sunshine!

It had been raining nonstop until now,

so not getting rained on alone is a win!

First, I headed to

Lisbon's fab lab.

Major cities around the world

have maker spaces

with 3D printers

and laser cutters.

I was thinking of engraving

Masu Photo sponsor logos onto FOMUS masu,

so I went to check it out,

but unfortunately, you need to book in advance

because the staff won't be there otherwise.

Too bad!

Can't do anything about that.

So from there, I went to scout souvenir shops.

Portugal's souvenirs are pretty much all the same.

Personally, I liked Porto better than Lisbon

as a city,

so for the new souvenir brand I'm creating,

even though there are fewer tourists, considering the cityscape,

it might be more interesting to place it in Porto.

This is something

you can't know without actually going,

but that was my gut feeling.

Plus, since this was the first non-rainy day in Lisbon, I walked to a spot with a great view.

Lisbon has so many stairs!

Still,

from above you can get a panoramic view like this.

If it were sunny, I'd be able to see even more,

but unfortunately it was cloudy.

Walking around Lisbon, what strikes me is

just how many hills there are.

Porto had lots of hills too,

but Lisbon has even more.

Lisbon is a city I'd love to visit as a tourist!

And to end the day, at a bar.

I had a cocktail for the first time in a while.

Going solo to a bar in an unfamiliar place

is something I actually enjoy.

The Portugal expedition comes to an end

With this,

my roughly 2-week Portugal expedition is over.

I traveled around Porto, Lisbon, and the Madeira Islands,

scouting wine producers and souvenir shops,

and connecting with local people.

Personally, the Madeira Islands exceeded my expectations,

and I felt it was a place where FOMUS could do something really interesting.

Also, I had plenty of solo time,

so I was able to rethink my schedule

for the second half of 2025

and how I spend my time.

Right now, FOMUS projects include:

- Updating the masu accessory line "FOMUS Parure,"

- Developing a new brand starting spring or later,

- Overseas masu sales, Masu Photo shoots

and more,

while in Japan,

we're also working on the Sagishima art gallery

with our team.

After this, a Nordic expedition starting in February was planned,

with Masu Photo shoots at each location,

but I've put that on hold.

I felt that adding more new projects

would make the current ones

half-baked.

Overseas, I'll focus on my personal grassroots activities

and building a stable foundation for FOMUS,

while in Japan,

I'll focus on the Sagishima art gallery!

For the masu accessory "FOMUS Parure,"

we're currently aligning designs and

checking whether the value proposition matches the price point.

For the souvenir-focused brand "Masu-Game"

planned to launch spring or later,

until the designs come in,

I'll be thinking hard about

how to deliver it and

where to deliver it.

Right now, I'm targeting tourist spots:

In Japan -- Asakusa, Kyoto, Hakone.

In Portugal -- Porto, Madeira.

In America -- LA, NY, and Hawaii.

Plus Dubai, Paris --

those are the areas I want to expand into.

Of course, I can't expand all at once,

so I'll proceed carefully one at a time,

and I'd like to do it through collaborations

with local creators.

By visiting tourist spots,

even in the off-season,

I can see that customers are still coming,

so with that in mind, I'll work on delivering.

All in all,

for the Portugal expedition, I feel I used my time effectively in Porto and Madeira.

Actually, I'd been sending emails to wine producers,

and one replied saying

they don't need masu from their end and won't sell them, but if I want to collaborate with Madeira wine, they'll introduce me to their Japanese distributor!

As expected, to get a completely unrelated country or traditional craft adopted, you need to deliver the meaning and story carefully, or people won't be interested.

Let's keep at it!

excerpt_en: So, the Portugal expedition is over, and I returned to Ireland at 9 AM. Since it was an early morning flight, I needed to be at the airport by 5 AM, so I didn't book a hotel and spent the night on an airport bench. Unfortunately,
body_en:
So, the Portugal expedition is over,

and I returned to Ireland at 9 AM.

Since it was an early morning flight,

I needed to be at the airport by 5 AM,

so I didn't book a hotel

and spent the night on an airport bench.

Unfortunately,

the airport lounge didn't open until 6 AM,

so I couldn't use it lol

The experience of living in Ireland

For Ireland,

I have a Working Holiday visa

and a residence card,

so entering the country is very smooth.

Also, coming back to Dublin city after a while,

there's a sense of relief.

It really reminded me that "staying somewhere as a traveler"

and "living somewhere" are

completely different.

This time I'll be at a hotel in Dublin city for about 4 nights.

Staying at a friend's place was an option,

but this time, I've been moving around a lot,

and coordinating schedules with friends to travel together would be difficult, so I went with a hotel.

I wanted to keep it as cheap as possible,

so I chose a 16-person mixed dormitory.

A room with 8 bunk beds lol

After 3 years of address-hopping,

my body can handle sleeping anywhere,

and having other people around is no problem. lol

Building stronger connections through frequency of contact

And today being Wednesday,

there was a Japanese meetup in the evening.

About 80 people gathered.

From long-term residents

to newcomers,

all sorts of people come,

and this is the best spot for connecting with Japanese people.

Being able to exchange all kinds of information is also a plus.

After about 3 and a half hours of socializing,

relationships with people I've known for a while get stronger,

and it gets across that MaSU is primarily based in Dublin.

Also, for new people,

I can talk about FOMUS,

and since I'm different from typical students or working holiday folks,

people find it interesting.

I guess it's a bit unusual.

This is how I build

friendships and relationships in Ireland.

In the end,

I believe everything comes down to "people,"

and the accumulation of opportunities to meet (frequency of contact)

builds trust,

so I attend whenever I can.

Plus, it's simply fun.

Also, during my address-hopping days,

I often visited

various communities,

and smoothly integrating myself among strangers

was a required skill.

Having developed this over 3 years,

I've become able to slip smoothly

into all kinds of communities.

This ability

was definitely acquired later in life,

but it's definitely better to have it.

So right after returning to Ireland,

I attended a social gathering.

Building connections is something money can't buy,

so I'll keep showing up in person and building good relationships!

title_en: Attending an Event at the Embassy of Japan in Ireland
excerpt_en: So, this evening, I received an invitation from the Embassy of Japan in Ireland and attended their event. This event is invitation-only, and not just anyone can participate.
body_en:
So, this evening,

I received an invitation from

the Embassy of Japan in Ireland

and attended their event.

This event is invitation-only,

and not just anyone can participate.

Basically,

the people invited are families of expats from major Japanese companies in Ireland, business owners operating in Ireland, and people doing Japan-related work in Ireland.

In other words, people on Working Holiday visas

don't get invited lol

I've only been in Ireland

for half a year since arriving,

but because I provided masu

at an embassy event in 2024,

I was invited this time

as a business owner in Ireland.

Apparently this kind of thing is quite rare,

and even long-term expats

were surprised. lol

From my perspective,

I can only stay in Ireland for one year,

so there's a time limit.

How far can I push my outreach in that one year?

That's the challenge,

so I need to move with a sense of urgency.

In 2025,

with the Osaka Expo and the morning TV drama serial,

it's a year when Ireland will be focusing on Japan.

In other words, Japan will be

getting media coverage.

The traditional Japanese craft of "masu"

is naturally part of that,

and I'm seriously going after this opportunity!

Also, starting this year,

the Ambassador to Ireland has changed.

I met the new one for the first time today,

and to be honest, he's quite elderly. lol

He'd apparently heard about me from the previous ambassador,

and already knew about the masu story,

which made me really happy.

I'm also looking into providing masu

at future embassy events,

so we discussed various things.

#AlwaysPrepared

FOMUS is a brand I started,

but many people are already involved in the projects, and it's no longer just my brand alone.

That's exactly why, as the one who came here alone

to expand it in Europe,

I need to connect with more people

than anyone else.

Since founding FOMUS,

I've lost all hesitation about diving solo

into established communities and groups.

Having developed the ability

to boldly jump right in

is incredibly important

for this kind of work.

Of course, not many Japanese people

are making moves like this,

so I don't expect

everyone to welcome me.

All the other men were in suits.

The women wore either kimono or

the kind of fancy outfits you'd wear to a wedding.

I was in a hoodie

and running shoes. lol

I don't dress the same as everyone else,

so I stood out in a weird way lol

I didn't bring a suit in the first place,

and my identity isn't the suited-up type.

I ignore rigid conventions

and sell "MaSU" as myself.

People exchange business cards following Japanese customs,

but I don't hand out business cards in Ireland.

I haven't even made any.

Instead of a business card,

I have something that makes

a far stronger impact --

the "masu."

I always wear a masu badge,

and it has overwhelmingly more impact

than any business card. lol

Branding really is

so important, I realize again.

In fact, even people meeting me for the first time

somehow already know me as "the masu guy,"

apparently I've become a rumor somewhere,

and they already know about me lol

Even in Ireland,

far from Japan,

the masu guy MaSU is going strong.

It was an extremely productive time.

Moving luggage too!

After the embassy event,

I took the train back to the city center.

On the train,

I happened to be with someone I'd spoken to at the event,

so we chatted on the way back.

And I still had an important mission

left to complete.

That was luggage transport.

Right now, I don't have a place in Dublin,

so all my stuff is in my suitcase

and a separate bag.

I'd been having a friend keep them at their place, but they didn't have much storage space, and my luggage was sticking out too much.

So I'd only had them hold it during the Portugal expedition,

and now I needed to move it to another friend's place.

That friend's place has plenty of storage,

and I knew from having them hold my suitcase briefly last year

that it wouldn't take up much room.

So, from one friend's place to another,

I had to haul my luggage.

That said, the distance was over 1 kilometer.

The weather was rain and wind.

So I reluctantly used an Uber

to move.

And got my luggage stored.

My current luggage is

my overseas travel set,

with my camera and everything still stored away.

The upcoming Nordic expedition

originally had photo shoots planned,

but the schedule is too tight,

carrying a camera while moving around for a month straight

would be too much,

and with multiple projects going on,

adding more would cause problems for others.

That's why I decided against it.

So that's how it went --

a day of making lots of connections at the embassy event while also moving my luggage for next week's expedition.

Apparently a massive storm is coming tomorrow,

so I plan to hunker down and work!
